skill.name: antipattern-catalog
skill.description: Create structured catalogs of framework anti-patterns, technical debt, severity levels, remediation guidance, and do-not-repeat checklists.
skill.summary: Catalog framework anti-patterns and turn findings into remediation guidance.
skill.author: Dowwie

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged Markdown examples and fenced templates as command execution, network use, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance. Manual review found no executable scripts, no prompt injection attempt, no data exfiltration path, and no operational network or shell behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged lines are Markdown inline code, Markdown fences, Python example snippets, and output templates. They document anti-patterns and remediation examples, but the skill contains no executable Ruby, shell, or command runner logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens appear inside Markdown tables or fenced documentation examples. No file contains executable code, shell invocation APIs, or instructions to run these snippets.

low_findings.title: HTTP Library Mention Is Documentation Only
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The requests.get reference is an example of a performance anti-pattern named Sync in Async. It is not executed and does not define a network destination or exfiltration behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 62 is a Markdown table row describing a blocking HTTP call as a code smell. There is no import, runtime code, URL, credential handling, or network call implementation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Have No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged lines contain frontmatter text, a template heading, and a related-skill reference. Manual review found no cryptographic algorithm, hashing routine, password handling, or encryption code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain crypto APIs or algorithm names in an executable context. The skill is a Markdown guide and does not implement security-sensitive computation.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Flags Are Unsupported
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited lines describe the skill purpose and a generic remediation placeholder. They do not request host enumeration, environment inspection, credential discovery, or system command output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no reconnaissance commands or prompts that ask the model to inspect the host system. The lines are documentation text within the skill instructions.

content.value_statement: Framework analysis can produce scattered notes that are hard to act on. This skill organizes observed anti-patterns into severity-ranked catalog entries with remediation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Categorizes anti-patterns as structural, behavioral, observability, or performance issues.
Assigns severity levels from critical to cosmetic based on documented impact.
Formats findings as repeatable catalog entries with description, impact, and remediation sections.
Turns framework analysis notes into a do-not-repeat checklist.
Provides examples for inheritance depth, silent failures, hidden LLM responses, and async blocking calls.
limitations: Does not scan repositories or execute code by itself.
Requires existing observations from analysis or review work.
Provides guidance, but human reviewers must confirm severity and priority.
Focuses on framework design patterns, not legal, compliance, or production incident analysis.

use_cases.title: Architecture Review Notes
use_cases.description: Convert framework review observations into a structured anti-pattern catalog for design decisions.

use_cases.title: Code Review Follow-Up
use_cases.description: Group repeated code smells by severity and create clear remediation actions for teams.

use_cases.title: Framework Comparison Lessons
use_cases.description: Synthesize lessons across multiple frameworks into patterns to avoid in future implementations.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Catalog
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an anti-pattern catalog from these framework observations. Group each issue by category and assign a severity level.

prompt_templates.title: Add Remediation Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these code review notes into catalog entries. Include description, impact, remediation, and a concise do-not-repeat checklist.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Frameworks
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these findings from several frameworks. Identify recurring anti-patterns and explain which design choices should not be repeated.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Technical Debt
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a severity-ranked anti-pattern catalog for leadership review. Separate critical risks, major maintainability issues, and lower-priority cleanup items.

output_examples.input: Observations mention deep inheritance, swallowed exceptions, and missing token metrics.
output_examples.output: A categorized catalog with structural, behavioral, and observability entries, each with severity, impact, and remediation guidance.

output_examples.input: A team needs lessons learned from comparing agent frameworks.
output_examples.output: A do-not-repeat checklist covering inheritance depth, error propagation, loop guards, shared state, and structured tool feedback.

output_examples.input: Review notes include blocking calls inside async functions and repeated configuration loading.
output_examples.output: A performance anti-pattern section that explains user impact and recommends async clients, batching, caching, and lazy loading.

best_practices: Start from concrete observations and preserve the framework context for each finding.
Use severity levels consistently so teams can prioritize remediation work.
Convert recurring issues into checklist items that future designs can verify.
anti_patterns: Using the catalog without evidence from actual code or framework behavior.
Treating every style issue as a critical architectural problem.
Leaving remediation vague instead of naming a safer pattern or design change.

faq.question: What does this skill produce?
faq.answer: It produces a structured anti-pattern catalog with categories, severity levels, impact notes, remediation guidance, and checklist items.

faq.question: Does it inspect source code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It organizes observations provided by the user or by earlier analysis steps.

faq.question: Which categories does it use?
faq.answer: It uses structural, behavioral, observability, and performance categories.

faq.question: Can it help prioritize technical debt?
faq.answer: Yes. It maps findings to critical, major, minor, and cosmetic severity levels.

faq.question: Is it only for agent frameworks?
faq.answer: No. The examples focus on agent frameworks, but the catalog format can support many software frameworks.

faq.question: What input should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide review notes, framework comparison results, code smell observations, or architecture analysis findings.
